q: The absolute minimum value, of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer function, in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, is
question: The absolute minimum value, of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer function, in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, is
solution:
fx=x2-x+1+x2-x+1  x∈-1,2

∵x2-x+1→x2-x+1>0

∴fx=x2-x+1+x2-x+1

Now,

Consider g(x)=x2-x+1

For the minimum value of g(x),

g'(x)=0⇒2x-1=0

⇒x=12

x2-x+1 attains its minimum value at x=12

And minx2-x+1=0 as x2-x+1>0

⇒fx attains its minimum at x=12

So, f12=34+0=34

q: If the function $f(x)=2 x^3-9 \mathrm{ax}^2+12 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{x}+1$, where $\mathrm{a} \gt 0$, attains its local maximum and local minimum values at $p$ and $q$, respectively, such that $\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q}$, then $f(3)$ is equal to:
question: If the function $f(x)=2 x^3-9 \mathrm{ax}^2+12 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{x}+1$, where $\mathrm{a} \gt 0$, attains its local maximum and local minimum values at $p$ and $q$, respectively, such that $\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q}$, then $f(3)$ is equal to:
solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=6 x^2-18 a x+12 a^2$
$f^{\prime}(x)=6\left(x^2-3 a x+2 a^2\right)$
roots are $a, 2 a$
$\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^2=2 \mathrm{a}$
$a=2$
$f(x)=2 x^3-18 x^2+48 x+1$
$f(3)=37$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a polynomial of degree three that has a local maximum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and a local minimum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a polynomial of degree three that has a local maximum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and a local minimum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Clearly p'(x)=λ(x-1)(x-2) where λ>0

Integrating both sides

px=λx33-3x22+2x+C

Given

p1=8⇒λ13-32+2+C=8  ⇒5λ6+C=8  ...1

p(2)=4⇒λ83-6+4+C=4  ⇒23λ+C=4  ...2

By 1 and 2 ⇒C=-12

⇒p(0)=-12

q: The slope of the line touching both the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>32</mn><mi>y</mi></math> is
question: The slope of the line touching both the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>32</mn><mi>y</mi></math> is
solution:
y2=41x

x2=4-8y

The equation of the tangent to y2=4x is y= mx+am
The equation of the tangent to x2=4by is  y= mx-bm2

Since, they have a common tangent,

      am=-bm2

∴   1m=8m2

∴   m3=18

∴   m=12

∴  The slope of the common tangent is 1 2 .

subject: Mathematics
q: The volume of the largest possible right circular cylinder that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius $=\sqrt{3}$ is:
question: The volume of the largest possible right circular cylinder that can be inscribed in a sphere of radius $=\sqrt{3}$ is:
solution:
Given, radius of sphere $=\sqrt{3}$ Now, In $\triangle \mathrm{OAB}$, by Pythagoras theorem $$ (\mathrm{OA})^2=(\mathrm{OB})^2+(\mathrm{AB})^2 $$
$$ \begin{aligned} &(\sqrt{3})^2=\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^2+r^2 \\ &3=\frac{h^2}{4}+r^2 \Rightarrow r^2=3-\frac{h^2}{4} \end{aligned} $$ Now, volume of cylinder $=\pi r^2 h$ $$ \begin{aligned} &V=\pi\left(3-\frac{h^2}{4}\right) h \\ &V=3 \pi h-\frac{\pi h^3}{4} \end{aligned} $$ Now, for largest possible right circular cylinder the volume must be maximum $\therefore$ For maximum volume, $\frac{d V}{d h}=0$ Now, Differentiating eq. (2) w.r.t. $h$ $V^1=\frac{d V}{d h}=3 \pi-\frac{3}{4} \pi h^2$ or $3 \pi-\frac{3}{4} \pi h^2=0 \Rightarrow 3 \pi=\frac{3}{4} \pi h^2$ $\Rightarrow h^2=4 \Rightarrow h=2$ Now, volume $(V)$ of the cylinder $$ =\pi\left(3-\frac{h^2}{4}\right) h=\pi(6-2)=4 \pi $$

subject: Mathematics
q: For the curve $\mathrm{y}=3 \sin \theta \cos \theta, \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{e}^\theta \sin \theta$, $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$, the tangent is parallel to $\mathrm{x}$-axis when $\theta$ is:
question: For the curve $\mathrm{y}=3 \sin \theta \cos \theta, \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{e}^\theta \sin \theta$, $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$, the tangent is parallel to $\mathrm{x}$-axis when $\theta$ is:
solution:
Given, $y=3 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3[\sin \theta(-\sin \theta)+\cos \theta(\cos \theta)] \\
&\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3\left[\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta\right]=3 \cos 2 \theta . \\
&\text { and } x=\mathrm{e}^\theta \sin \theta \\
&\frac{d x}{d \theta}=e^\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta e^\theta \\
&\frac{d x}{d \theta}=e^\theta(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)
\end{aligned}
$$
Dividing (1) by (2)
$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 \cos 2 \theta}{e^\theta(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)}=\frac{3\left(\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta\right)}{e^\theta(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)} \\
\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{e^\theta(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)} \\
\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{e^\theta}
\end{gathered}
$$
Given tangent is parallel to $x$-axis then
$\frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$0=\frac{3(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{e^\theta}$
or $\cos \theta-\sin \theta=0 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow \tan \theta=1 \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{\tan \pi}{4} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$

q: If the tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>y</mi></math> at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is parallel to the line joining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
question: If the tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>y</mi></math> at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is parallel to the line joining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
solution:
y=x+siny

dydx=1+cosydydx

dydx=11-cosy

Now for a, b, dydx=11-cosb

As the tangent is parallel to the line joining 0, 32 and 12, 2

 ⇒11-cosb=2-3212-0

⇒  cosb=0  ⇒  b=2n+1π2, n∈I

Point lie on curve b=a+sinb

b-a=sinb

|b-a|=1

q: The triangle of maximum area that can be inscribed in a given circle of radius '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>' is :
question: The triangle of maximum area that can be inscribed in a given circle of radius '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>' is :
options: An equilateral triangle having each of its side of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mi>r</mi></math>.
An isosceles triangle with base equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math>.

A right angle triangle having two of its sides of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>.
solution:
h=rsinθ+r

base =BC=2rcosθ

θ∈0,π2

Area of ΔABC=12BC.h

Δ=122rcosθ.rsinθ+r

=r2cosθ.1+sinθ

dΔdθ=r2cosθddθ1+sinθ+1+sinθddθcosθ

dΔdθ=r2cosθ0+cosθ+1+sinθ-sinθ

dΔdθ=r2cos2θ-sinθ-sin2θ

dΔdθ=r21-sin2θ-sinθ-sin2θ

=r21-sinθ-2sin2θ

=r2-2sin2θ-sinθ+1

=r2-2sin2θ-2sinθ+sinθ+1

=r2-2sinθsinθ+1+1sinθ+1

=r21+sinθ⏟positive1-2sinθ=0

⇒θ=π6

⇒ Δ is maximum where θ=π6

Δmax=334r2= area of equilateral Δ with BC=3r.

q: The maximum area (in sq. units) of a rectangle having its base on the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> axis and its other two vertices on the parabola, <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> such that the rectangle lies inside the parabola, is :
question: The maximum area (in sq. units) of a rectangle having its base on the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> axis and its other two vertices on the parabola, <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> such that the rectangle lies inside the parabola, is :
solution:
Since, given parabola is symmetric about the y- axis, hence rectangle will also be symmetric about y- axis.

Let one vertex of the rectangle on the x- axis be α,0, then

Area of rectangle A=2α.12-α2

Differentiating both sides with respect to α, we get

⇒dAdα=24-6α2=0⇒α=2, -2

For area to be maximum, put α=2 in the equation for area of the rectangle, we get

Amax=2×2×12-22=32

subject: Mathematics
q:
For the function $f(x)=(\cos x)-x+1, x \in \mathbb{R}$, between the following two statements
(S1) $f(x)=0$ for only one value of $x$ in $[0, \pi]$.
(S2) $f(x)$ is decreasing in $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ and increasing in $\left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right]$.
question:
For the function $f(x)=(\cos x)-x+1, x \in \mathbb{R}$, between the following two statements
(S1) $f(x)=0$ for only one value of $x$ in $[0, \pi]$.
(S2) $f(x)$ is decreasing in $\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ and increasing in $\left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \pi\right]$.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-x+1 \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=-\sin x-1\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{f}$ is decreasing $\forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=0 \\ & \mathrm{f}(0)=2, \mathrm{f}(\pi)=-\pi\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{f}$ is strictly decreasing in $[0, \pi]$ and $\mathrm{f}(0) . \mathrm{f}(\pi) < 0$
$\Rightarrow$ only one solution of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=0$
$\mathrm{S} 1$ is correct and $\mathrm{S} 2$ is incorrect.

q: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math><mi>g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>∀</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ϵ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> . Then the set of all <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ϵ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> , where the function <math><mi>h</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>f</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is increasing, is:
question: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> and <math><mi>g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>∀</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ϵ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> . Then the set of all <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">ϵ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> , where the function <math><mi>h</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>f</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is increasing, is:
solution:
hx=fogx=egx-gx
=ex2-x-x2-x
h'x=ex2-x×2x-1-2x+1
=2x-1ex2-x-1
∵hx is increasing ∴h'x≥0 ⇒2x-1ex2-x-1≥0
Case 1: 2x-1≥0 & x2-x≥0
⇒x≥12 & x∈-∞, 0∪1,∞⇒x∈1, ∞
Case 2: 2x-1≤0 and x2-x≤0
x<12 and x∈0, 1 ⇒x∈0,12
∴  x∈0,12∪[1,∞)

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of points, where the curve y=x5-20x3+50x+2 crosses the x-axis, is _____.
question: The number of points, where the curve y=x5-20x3+50x+2 crosses the x-axis, is _____.
solution:
Let, f(x)=x5-20x3+50x+2

Now differentiating the function fx with respect to x we get,

 f'(x)=5x4-60x2+50

⇒f'(x)=5x4-12x2+10

Now equating f'(x)=0 we get,

x4-12x2+10=0

⇒x2=12±144-402

⇒x2=6±26

⇒x2≈11.1, 0.9

⇒x≈±3.31, ±0.95

Now finding the nature of function at integer value near by above value we get,

f0=2, f1>0, f2<0, f-1<0 & f-2>0

Now plotting the graph by observing above values, we get,

Hence, it will cross x-axis 5 times.

q: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>-</mo><mo>|</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>|</mo></math> and <math><mi>g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> <math><mi>x</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>∈</mo><mi> </mi><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> attains maximum value at <math><mi>α</mi></math> and <math><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> attains minimum value at <math><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><munder><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mrow></munder><mfrac><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to
question: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>-</mo><mo>|</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>|</mo></math> and <math><mi>g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> <math><mi>x</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>∈</mo><mi> </mi><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> attains maximum value at <math><mi>α</mi></math> and <math><mi>g</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> attains minimum value at <math><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><munder><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mrow></munder><mfrac><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> is equal to
solution:
Maxima of fx will occur at x=2 i.e., α=2

Minima of g(x) will occur at x=-1 i.e. β=-1

∴ limx→2(x-1)(x-2)(x-3)(x-2)(x-4)=12.

subject: Mathematics
q: The tangent at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> to the curve, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> does not pass through the point:
question: The tangent at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> to the curve, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> does not pass through the point:
solution:
Given curve x2y2-2x=4-4y

Differentiate both sides w.r.t  x,

⇒ 2xy2+2y.x2.dydx-2=-4.dydx

⇒ dydx2y.x2+4=2-2x.y2

⇒ dydx2,-2=2-2×2×42-2×4+4=1412=76

Equation of tangent, y+2=76x-2

⇒7x-6y=26, which doesn't pass through -2-7.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the absolute maximum value of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>180</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></mfenced></msup></math>in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>α</mi></mfenced></math>, then
question: If the absolute maximum value of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>180</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></mfenced></msup></math>in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>α</mi></mfenced></math>, then
solution:
Given,

fx=x2-2x+7e4x3-12x2-180x+31

Now differentiating the function to find function is increasing or decreasing,

f'x=e4x3-12x2-180x+3112x2-2x-15+2x-1

⇒f'x=e4x3-12x2-180x+3112x+3x-5+2x-1

⇒f'x<0 for x∈-3,0

So, fxis decreasing function on -3,0

The absolute maximum value of the function fx is at x=-3

⇒α=-3

subject: Mathematics
q: Let a curve y=fx, x∈0, ∞ pass through the points P1, 32 and Qa, 12. If the tangent at any point R(b, f(b)) to the given curve cuts the y-axis at the point S(0, c) such that bc=3, then PQ2 is equal to _____.
question: Let a curve y=fx, x∈0, ∞ pass through the points P1, 32 and Qa, 12. If the tangent at any point R(b, f(b)) to the given curve cuts the y-axis at the point S(0, c) such that bc=3, then PQ2 is equal to _____.
solution:
Given,

A curve y=fx, x∈0, ∞ pass through the points P1, 32 and Qa, 12,

And the tangent at any point R(b, f(b)) to the given curve cuts the y-axis at the point S(0, c) such that bc=3,

Now tangent to the curve is given by,

Y-y=mX-x, m=dydx

Now at Y-axis, put X=0 we get,

Y=y-mx or c=y-mx as point S0,c given,

Now given bc=3

⇒xy-mx=3 {here b=x on the curve}

⇒y-xdydx=3x

⇒ydx-xdyx2=3dxx·1x2

⇒d-yx=3dx-2-2

Now integrating both side we get,

⇒yx=32x2+C, 

Now given curve passes through P1,32, so C=0

So, equation of curve will be 2xy=3

Now curve also passes through the point Qa,12

So, α=3

Hence, P1, 32, Q3, 12

So, by distance formula we get, PQ2=4+1=5

subject: Mathematics
q: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
question: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}(\cos x-\sin x)$
$=\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}$
$f(x)$ is increasing if $-\frac{\pi}{2} < x+\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\pi}{2}$
$-\frac{3 \pi}{4} < x < \frac{\pi}{4}$
hence $f(x)$ is increasing when $x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let A=aij be a 3×3 matrix, where aij=1, if i=j-x, if i-j=12x+1, otherwise 
Let a function f:R→R be defined as fx=detA. Then the sum of maximum and minimum values of f on R is equal to:
question:
Let A=aij be a 3×3 matrix, where aij=1, if i=j-x, if i-j=12x+1, otherwise 
Let a function f:R→R be defined as fx=detA. Then the sum of maximum and minimum values of f on R is equal to:
solution:
We have,

aij=1, if i=j-x, if i-j=12x+1, otherwise 

Then,

A=1−x2x+1−x1−x2x+1−x1

⇒  A=1-x2+x-x+2x2+x+2x+1x2-2x-1

⇒A=1-x2+2x3+2x3-4x2-2x+x2-2x-1

⇒A=fx=4x3-4x2-4x

⇒f'x=43x2−2x−1

For maxima or minima, f'x=0

⇒  3x2−2x−1=0

⇒  x=1, −13

Now,

f''x=46x-2

f''1=46·1-2=16>0( point of minima)

f''-13=46·-13-2=-16<0 ( point of maxima)

Hence, required sum is

f1+f-13

=-4+2027=−8827

q:
The maximum value of z in the following equation z=6xy+y2, where 3x+4y≤100 and 4x+3y≤75 for x≥0 and y≥0
is
question:
The maximum value of z in the following equation z=6xy+y2, where 3x+4y≤100 and 4x+3y≤75 for x≥0 and y≥0
is
solution:
z=6xy+y2=y(6x+y)

3x+4y≤100 …i

4x+3y≤75 …ii

x≥0

y≥0

x≤75-3y4

Given z=y(6x+y)

⇒z≤y6·75-3y4+y

⇒z≤12225y-7y2

Now range of 12225y-7y2 is (-∞,(225)22×4×7]

So, z≤12225y-7y2≤(225)22×4×7

=5062556

≈904.0178

≈904.02

It will be attained at y=22514

subject: Mathematics
q: The shortest distance between line $y-x=1$ and curve $x=y^2$ is
question: The shortest distance between line $y-x=1$ and curve $x=y^2$ is
solution:
$$
P=\left(y^2, y\right)
$$
Perpendicular distance from $P$ to $x-y+1=0$ is $\frac{\left|y^2-y+1\right|}{\sqrt{2}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{y}^2-\mathrm{y}+1>0 \forall \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{R} \\
& \therefore \text { Coefficient } \mathrm{y}^2>0 \\
& \therefore \text { Min value }=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{ac}-\mathrm{b}^2}{4 \mathrm{a}}\right)=\frac{3}{4 \sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

q: The local maximum value of the function, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></mfenced><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is
question: The local maximum value of the function, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac></mfenced><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is
solution:
f'(x)=0 for maximum value
Let y=2xx2

lny=x2ln2x

1yy'=2xln2x+x212x×-2x2

y'=(xy)2ln2x-1

y'=2xx2x2ln2x-1

2ln2x=1

2x=e12

x=2e-12

Then maximum value will be
f2e-12=22e-124e-1=e2e-1=e2e

q: The normal to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The normal to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
options: Meets the curve again in the fourth quadrant
Does not meet the curve again
Meets the curve again in the second quadrant
Meets the curve again in the third quadrant
solution:
Given x2+2xy-3y2=0

x+3yx-y=0

Pair of straight lines passing through the origin.

∵ x+3y=0  or  x-y=0

Normal exists at 1, 1 which is on x-y=0

⇒   The slope of normal at 1, 1=-1 

∴   Equation of normal will be

y-1=-x-1    y-y1=mx-x1

y-1=-x+1

x+y=2

Now, find the point of intersection with x+3y=0. 

   x+y=2x+3y=0
  ----------------
   -2y=2   ⇒y=-1, x=3

∵ 3, -1 lies in the fourth quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
q: $\max_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16\sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to:
question: $\max_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16\sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $y = \dfrac{x}{2}$. Since $0 \leq x \leq \pi$, we have $0 \leq y \leq \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

The given expression becomes $f(y) = 16\sin y \cos^3 y$.

Differentiating with respect to $y$:

$f'(y) = 16(\cos y \cdot \cos^3 y + \sin y \cdot 3\cos^2 y(-\sin y))$

$f'(y) = 16\cos^2 y(\cos^2 y - 3\sin^2 y)$

For maximum value, $f'(y) = 0$:

$\cos^2 y - 3\sin^2 y = 0 \Rightarrow \tan^2 y = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Since $y \in \left[0, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$, $\tan y = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

Substituting $y = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ in $f(y)$:

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 16\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 16 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^3$

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 8 \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} = 3\sqrt{3}$

The maximum value is $3\sqrt{3}$.

Answer: $3\sqrt{3}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> be respectively the absolute maximum and the absolute minimum values of the function, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>]</mo></math> . Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>m</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> be respectively the absolute maximum and the absolute minimum values of the function, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>12</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>]</mo></math> . Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>m</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given fx=2x3-9x2+12x+5, x∈0,3

Differentiating w.r.t x,

⇒f′x=6x2-18x+12

⇒f′x=6x-1x-2

For maxima/minima f'x=0

⇒6x-1x-2=0⇒x=1,2

Now, f"x=12x-18

So at x=1, f"x<0

and at x=2, f"x>0

So, for absolute maxima/minima

⇒f1=10

⇒f2=9

⇒f0=5

⇒f3=14

⇒M=14

⇒m=5

∴ M-m=9

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> be a twice differentiable function on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>≥</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>≥</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo></math> for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> be a twice differentiable function on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mn>2</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>≥</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>≥</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo></math> for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then :
solution:
Given f'x≥1

⇒   ∫25f'xdx≥∫251.dx

⇒fx25≥x25

⇒f5−f2≥3

⇒f5≥11      ....1

Now f''x≥4⇒∫25f''xdx≥∫254dx

=f'x25≥4x25

⇒   f'5−f'2≥12

⇒   f'5≥17    ....2

1+2⇒f5+f'5≥28.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the function $f(x)=\frac{x}{3}+\frac{3}{x}+3, x \neq 0$ be strictly increasing in $\left(-\infty, \alpha_1\right) \mathrm{U}\left(\alpha_2, \infty\right)$ and strictly decreasing in $\left(\alpha_3, \alpha_4\right) \mathrm{U}\left(\alpha_4, \alpha_5\right)$. Then $\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^5 \alpha_{\mathrm{i}}^2$ is equal to :-
question: Let the function $f(x)=\frac{x}{3}+\frac{3}{x}+3, x \neq 0$ be strictly increasing in $\left(-\infty, \alpha_1\right) \mathrm{U}\left(\alpha_2, \infty\right)$ and strictly decreasing in $\left(\alpha_3, \alpha_4\right) \mathrm{U}\left(\alpha_4, \alpha_5\right)$. Then $\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^5 \alpha_{\mathrm{i}}^2$ is equal to :-
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{x}{3}+\frac{3}{x}+3, x \neq 0 \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{3}-\frac{3}{x^2}=0 \quad \Rightarrow x= \pm 3 \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x^2-3}{3 x^2}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \gt 0 \forall(-\infty,-3) \cup(3, \infty) \rightarrow \text { increasing } \\ & \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \lt 0 \forall(-3,0) \cup(0,3) \rightarrow \text { decreasing }\end{aligned}$
$\sum_{i=1}^5 \alpha_i^2=(-3)^2+(3)^2+(-3)^2+(0)^2+(3)^2$
$=36$

subject: Mathematics
q: The maximum area of a right angled triangle with hypotenuse $h$ is :
question: The maximum area of a right angled triangle with hypotenuse $h$ is :
solution:
Let base $=b$
Altitude (or perpendicular) $=\sqrt{h^2-b^2}$ Area, $\mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{2} \times$ base $\times$ altitude $=\frac{1}{2} \times b \times \sqrt{h^2-b^2}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~A}}{d b}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\sqrt{h^2-b^2}+b \cdot \frac{-2 b}{2 \sqrt{h^2-b^2}}\right]$ $=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{h^2-2 b^2}{\sqrt{h^2-b^2}}\right]$ Put $\frac{d \mathrm{~A}}{d b}=0, \Rightarrow b=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}$ Maximumarea $=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{h}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{h^2-\frac{h^2}{2}}=\frac{h^2}{4}$

question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo>"</mo></msup><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is decreasing in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and increasing in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></math> is
solution:
Given: g(x)=3fx3+f(3-x) and f"(x)>0∀x∈(0,3)

⇒f'(x) is increasing function

⇒g'(x)=3×13·f'x3-f'(3-x)

⇒g'(x)=f'x3-f'(3-x)

It is given that g is decreasing in 0, α then g'(x)<0.

⇒f'x3-f'(3-x)<0

⇒f'x3<f'(3-x)

⇒x3<3-x

⇒x<9-3x

⇒x<94

⇒α=94

⇒8α=8×94

⇒8α=18

Volume, V=13πr2h

⇒V=13π9-h2h=13π9h-h3

dvdh=13π9-3h2

dvdh=0⇒h=3

d2Vdh2=13π-6h<0

∴ at h=3 , cone has maximum volume

∴ Vmax=13π93-33=23πcm3

subject: Mathematics
q: Area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ is
question: Area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ is
solution: Area of rectangle $A B C D=(2 a\cos \theta)$ $(2 b \sin \theta)=2 a b \sin 2 \theta$ $\Rightarrow$ Area of greatest rectangle is equal to $2 a b$ when $\sin 2 \theta=1$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following three statements for the function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x)=\left|\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right|-|x-1|$ :
(I) $f$ is differentiable at all $x>0$.
(II) $f$ is increasing in $(0,1)$.
(III) $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Then.
question:
Consider the following three statements for the function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x)=\left|\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right|-|x-1|$ :
(I) $f$ is differentiable at all $x>0$.
(II) $f$ is increasing in $(0,1)$.
(III) $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Then.
solution:
For $0 < x < 1$: $f(x) = -\ln x + x - 1$, $f'(x) = -\frac{1}{x} + 1 = \frac{x-1}{x} < 0$ (decreasing).
For $x > 1$: $f(x) = \ln x - x + 1$, $f'(x) = \frac{1}{x} - 1 = \frac{1-x}{x} < 0$ (decreasing).
At $x = 1$: LHD $= \frac{1-1}{1} = 0$, RHD $= \frac{1-1}{1} = 0$. So $f$ is differentiable at $x = 1$.
(I) TRUE: $f$ is differentiable for all $x > 0$.
(II) FALSE: $f$ is decreasing in $(0, 1)$.
(III) TRUE: $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Only (I) and (III) are TRUE.
